speaker: interviewee
text: The first year, I don't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living in New York. I was living in the Bronx, in Brooklyn, and White Plain

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Long Island. And from Whitestone, I went here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it different than New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: The difference?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now, it's big difference, because you can survive in New York, or you make it. And I'll be here still [INAUDIBLE] New York. We will be in New York, I couldn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Is it-- Today is July 20, 1995. My name is Mahli, M-A-H-L-I, Lieblich, L-I-E-B-L-I-C-H. We're interviewing Samuel, S-A-M-U-E-L, Klasner, K-L-A-S-N-E-R, in Sunrise, Florida. The language spoken is English. And it's tape number one. So

speaker: interviewee
text: Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hi, my name is Mahli, M-A-H-L-I, Lieblich, L-I-E-B-L-I-C-H. Today, I am interviewing Samuel Klasner, K-L-A-S-N-E-R. We're in Sunrise, Florida. It is July 20, 1995. Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Samuel Klasner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I'm born? I'm born September 8, 1917.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what town were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolbrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your earliest memories of Wolbrom.

speaker: interviewee
text: We was a lot of Jewish people there. Well, we had were friends. It was almost we was living together, like a family. We belonged to organizations, like [NON-ENGLISH]. We belonged to different organizations. There were a lot of, in Wolbrom, organizations, Zionist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the community at large, a Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately, how many Jews lived in Wolbrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was living, about 5,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jozef

speaker: interviewer
text: Klasner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klasner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bella Klasner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name is [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody else besides, your mother and father, living in your household with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was-- we was six children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the oldest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was born after Wolf?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Wolf was born Lola, my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola? Do you know approximately what year she was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have Adolf.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was an older brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was older than me, but he's the third one. Then was Bernard. That's the, the fourth. Then was my sister, Rachel. And me somewhere [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: From your five brothers and sisters, did any others survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about what it was like being a, a child in Wolbrom. You said you interacted with your neighbors. Was there any anti-Semitism that you felt as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not with the young, young, young generation. The old generation was difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how old were you when you realized that the older generation were different than the younger generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 18, 20 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was [INAUDIBLE] business.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had his own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of business was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a grocery business, you know. All kind like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the street of the store? [NON-ENGLISH] And what did your mother do? Did she just stay home, or did she help your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that your father died when you were young.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother support herself after your father was gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot of things helped, the brothers and the sister sent money to my mother. She could survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were already out of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of jobs did they work at? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have a clothing business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they in the same town, or did they move? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: They moved. They was not in the same town. The same town, you couldn't have a business. You couldn't survive there. When you went, you went to [NON-ENGLISH], Polish. And over there used to be a lot of factories. And people went out and bought stuff and pay out. Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is where your brothers went to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, where my brother was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first experience outside of Wolbrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Special experience from Wolbrom, I said it last time. I don't want to let my son go there because I was afraid. They, they find a Jew, they, they kill him. And what are you going to have it? Nothing. And he don't want to listen. He wants to go. He wants to see his father, where he lived, and his grandparents, where they lived. And so he went. And he saw some pictures. They showed to him. And he make pictures, the building where I lived, the street--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- what was the street you lived on in Wolbrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: What the street is named?

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the number of the building?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of apartment was it? Was it large? Was it small?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- medium.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many bedrooms did it have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had two bedrooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it on the first floor, second floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one, one, one flat floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of kitchen did your mother cook in? Did it have a stove? Or did it have an oven?

speaker: interviewee
text: The old fashioned. The old fashioned.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of old-fashioned kitchen was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was [? made ?] of wood, coal. And that's what make-- they cook over there in this kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you orthodox in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you more reform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reform.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shabbats. Like in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Shabbat. They would light the candles. We eat the challah and the fish, chicken. But not everybody could have this. It was very difficult because it was not a big city. What-- can't be factories and people go to work there. When they went to work, they went to work just a couple people on a job. But you couldn't do nothing with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a hard city to making a living in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- you see, over there the people, [INAUDIBLE] satisfied with this, but they make it, what they can spend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, did it have a special name?

speaker: interviewee
text: They call it public school. They don't-- not-- United States, you have a name. Any school would have it. They got a name. Over there, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there are other children besides Jewish children going to this school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Mix.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a mixture? How many students in a class, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was about 30, 30 students.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were some of the subjects that you were taught? Was it social studies? Was it a mixture of religious studies with--

speaker: interviewee
text: No religious studies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a straight public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Straight public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved within any organizations after school as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was in Jabotinsky's Zionists. I used to be a young, a young-- like over here, people, the, the kids, they started off with the four, five years, or six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you go for?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was for the weekend.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went every weekend?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't went. It was in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was not out of town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years did you go to [? Jabotinsky's ?] organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started off very young. I was maybe about six, seven years old, eight years old. And to to the end of the war, I was in the organization. I almost went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the baby of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the boys. Who was your favorite brother or sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was all the same thing, you know? They will care for the family, and we cared for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have a favorite brother or sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not special. Sister, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one, the picture that I have it. Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rachel. She was the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she did not survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went with her mother, together. [SIGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you the first time that you remember hearing the name Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 15, 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, in this city where my brothers and sister were, it's not far away from the German border. And they chased out the Jewish from, from the German, back to Poland, because they left Poland to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they chased them out, and they don't-- and Poland don't let them in. And the German doesn't let them in. They are in the middles. And they have a very, very hard time to let them into Poland. Oh, they came to Krakow, Warsaw, big cities. What year was this approximately, do you recall?

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were your first experiences with hearing about Hitler?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. We, we can-- we took a, a car and we fill in food. We bought it to the border there, where the Jewish people were sitting, you know? And we give it food to them because the German doesn't give them food. They hold them without food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you assisted these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot, a lot was. A few hundred. A few hundred of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ended up happening to these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, very close to the war started, they let them free.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans let them go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were approximately 15, 16 when you start hearing about Hitler. How did it affect your daily life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was very scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the family make any precautions or talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not special.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you realize, or when did your family realize, that Hitler was a threat to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not everybody realized this. Most people, most Jews, saw when they chased them out, and they saw what they do with them. Then they was scared, very scared. And they have no choice. Some people left, went to the United States. Some went to Israel, when they could, when they got the money to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to those who were left behind, who didn't have the money to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He killed them. Put them in, in concentration, and they killed them.

speaker: interviewee
text: What were you doing at the age of 15 and 16? Well, I was learning as a carpenter, because I want to go to Israel. So were you saving money?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to have it, a-- something that I can survive in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: A trade?

speaker: interviewee
text: A trade, yeah. So I was like to go, but they close up and they don't let that go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you after the age of 16? Were you still working as a carpenter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I went to my brothers over there, and I helped them out too. So I should, I should make it some-- a little more money. We should have it to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the clothing business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at what age did you become drafted into the Polish army?

speaker: interviewer
text: So between the ages of 16 and 20, did you work with your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you feel the pressure of the war coming closer and closer?

speaker: interviewee
text: They drafted me to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what that meant to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we-- I was a Jabotinsky. We used to have it the same schools, like the army's. Because we want to go to Israel, we want to fight for our country. But still, a lot of them, of them, we couldn't go. In 1939, end of 1939, when I was in the army until 1939-- no, almost 1940. End of 1939, we lost it. The Polish army was like-- they lose it. They couldn't do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the Army at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What position--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was shot here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were shot in the head?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little there. I have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you shot by? The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What rank did you hold?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What rank in the army did you hold? Were you just a private? A [NON-ENGLISH] soldier. They don't give me a chance to grow. What-- how did they treat you in the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the beginning, sometimes we had some of them from Wolbrom, very orthodox boys, you know? Religious. So they were in the army, and the food they don't eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was not kosher. So [INAUDIBLE]. So I went. I give them bread, and they give me the soup. I changed. Because I want them to have it. In the other way, you couldn't survive. You don't have nothing to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they make-- made no provisions for Jewish soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not special, no-- not special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they treat Jewish soldiers differently than the general soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We had-- one fellow, we had it, that's the-- was a soldier, and he was very religious. He didn't shoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to him when it came time to do battle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I will stand up for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean, that you stood up for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I don't let him do what they want to do to them. I was fighting with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you fighting with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Polish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people who were in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the army, what happened to you in the army? Where did they take you when they drafted you? What was the name of the town? Did they take--

speaker: interviewee
text: They draft me in Wolbrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Wolbrom, and they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Still, because I'm born in Wolbrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their procedure after they took you in Wolbrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent me to the arms camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in [NON-ENGLISH]. The German, German border's around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they trained you to do battle?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you do battle with the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- there was Friday morning in September, [INAUDIBLE]. There were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They take you. They send you papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to come to this and this city, and you're drafted to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's not everybody from Wolbrom went in one, one place. They split. So it was very hard to do this. But for me, it was more easy because I belonged to Jabotinsky, and they teach me the rules and everything for the soldiers So I have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was easier.

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't know what I know, and I was on top.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your first experience from-- being away from home for an extended period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you miss your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you write your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the army for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was till 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you fight against the Germans? I

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- we don't fight too much because they, they, they will have ammunition and everything. That's-- we don't-- couldn't-- we couldn't fight them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had no technology, no supplies?

speaker: interviewee
text: No nothing. Just a, a, a gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that battle take before you were overrun by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there, a few days. And then they took us in prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you to prison, to what prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the army prison, army prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was prisoner of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoner of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first [INAUDIBLE], they don't you food. You was hungry all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you a prisoner of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive as a prisoner of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you get used-- and when you're not eating, you're on a diet. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Over there, you don't have to be on a diet. You don't eat. You don't have it. They got a piece of bread and a little black coffee. Soup, we had a little soup, barley soup or something like this, you know? Did they know you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they separate you and treat you differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I wasn't-- not too long, because they have soldiers from, from the Russian German occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they belong-- they belong there. And it's Lemberg, when they kept the prisons. So they took it, and they set it. [INAUDIBLE] can go free who lives in Lemberg or in a different l there. So I tell them that I, I belong to Lemberg. That you came from the town of Lemberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So they'll free me.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't check? They just took your word for it? No. that's Right. Because I don't look like I was a Jew. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You have beautiful eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they, they let me go. So I went to Lemberg, and I was in Lemberg a couple months. And people from my town, from Wolbrom, came to Lemberg, and they told me that my mother, she-- somebody tell her that I'm dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you weren't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was fighting to go back. And I went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Lemberg from Wolbrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about, about five, six hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
